mnemonics.title: Exception Safety Levels
mnemonics.content: BAS-N: Basic, Strong, Nothrow - Remember as 'BAsic, Strong, Nothrow' or 'Basic And Strong, Never throw' to remember all three levels of exception safety guarantees.

mnemonics.title: Catch Block Order
mnemonics.content: M&M: Most Derived First, Most General Last - In catch blocks, catch Most derived (specific) classes first, then Most general (base) classes last.

mnemonics.title: Exception Hierarchy
mnemonics.content: ELR: Exception -> Logic_error -> Runtime_error - Remember as 'Every Learner Reads' to recall the top-level exception hierarchy.

mnemonics.title: Never Throw in Destructor
mnemonics.content: NDT: Never Destructors Throw - Remember 'Never During Termination' - destructors should never throw as it leads to terminate().

acronyms.usage: Pattern where resources are acquired in constructors and released in destructors, working seamlessly with exception handling for automatic cleanup.
Used by catch blocks to determine the actual type of exception object for proper handling in class hierarchies.
Declares that a function will not throw, enabling optimizations and required for move constructors and swap functions.
Uses exception handling internally for operations like vector::push_back and provides standard exception classes.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a pyramid with throw at bottom, calling functions above it. As exception rises (like water), each level checks catch blocks. If no match, it continues rising until caught or program terminates.
visualTips.purpose: Understanding how exceptions traverse the call stack upward until handled.

visualTips.visualization: Think of catch blocks as different-sized nets. The most specific (derived) is a small fine net at top, base class is a large net below. Exception falls into the first net that fits it.
visualTips.purpose: Visualizing why catch order matters in class hierarchies.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine peeling an onion layer by layer. Each function call is a layer. When exception occurs, layers are peeled off (stack frames removed), calling destructors (cleanup) at each layer before reaching the handler.
visualTips.purpose: Understanding automatic cleanup during exception propagation.

visualTips.visualization: A sandwich with bread (base) and filling (derived). When you take only the bread (slice), you lose the filling. Catching by value takes only base part, losing derived class information.
visualTips.purpose: Remembering why catching by reference prevents data loss.

analogies.analogy: Like a family tree: std::exception is the grandparent, std::runtime_error and std::logic_error are parents, and specific errors like std::bad_alloc are children. You can catch any descendant by catching an ancestor.
Like emergency evacuation: when something goes wrong (exception), each floor (function) clears its area (calls destructors for local objects) before people move to safer ground (exception handler).
Like catching a fragile message in a box (reference) rather than making a photocopy (by value). The original message with all details is preserved, not a stripped-down copy.
Like receiving a complaint, handling what you can (partial processing), then passing it to management (outer handler) for final resolution.
